overview:
In CS106A, Graphics and Animation introduce you to event-driven programming and visual problem solving using the ACM Graphics library. You work in a GraphicsProgram, add graphical objects (GObject) like GRect, GOval, and GLabel to a canvas, set their colors and layering, and position them using a 2D coordinate system with the origin at the top-left. You’ll also learn to load images, group shapes, and display text to create interactive visual programs.

Animation is built around a simple game loop: update object state (e.g., position), render, and pause for a short time step to control frame rate. Combining this with mouse and keyboard listeners lets you build interactive projects like bouncing balls, drag-and-drop interfaces, simple arcade games (Breakout), and data visualizations. You’ll practice timing, collision detection, and incremental design to keep programs smooth, responsive, and easy to debug.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary:
GraphicsProgram provides a drawing canvas where you add and manipulate GObject instances (e.g., GRect, GOval, GLine, GImage, GLabel). The coordinate system has (0,0) at the top-left; x increases to the right, y increases downward. You can place objects with add(obj, x, y), move them using setLocation or move(dx, dy), and control appearance with setColor, setFilled, and setFillColor. Z-order determines which objects appear on top; you can adjust it with sendToFront/sendToBack.

Animation in CS106A typically uses a loop with small movement steps and pause(milliseconds) to throttle speed and yield to the event system. Velocity variables (dx, dy) encode motion; you update position then reflect or clamp when hitting boundaries. Collision detection ranges from point sampling (e.g., getElementAt(x,y)) to bounding-box checks via getBounds() on GObject and GRectangle intersection tests. Interactivity is handled through mouse and keyboard listeners: addMouseListeners/addKeyListeners plus methods like mousePressed, mouseDragged, and keyPressed.

Images (GImage) and groups (GCompound) enable richer scenes and modular composition: build complex sprites by combining primitives into a single movable unit. Robust programs separate constants (sizes, speeds) from logic, and follow an incremental workflow—draw, move, then handle collisions and input—while using debugging aids like outlines, labels, and color changes.
learningObjectives: Create a GraphicsProgram and add, position, and style shapes, text, and images on the canvas.
Animate objects using a time-stepped loop with pause, managing velocities and frame rates.
Implement collision detection using point sampling, object bounds, and simple reflection/bounce logic.
Handle mouse and keyboard events to enable dragging, clicking, and character or paddle control.
Compose scenes with GCompound and manage layering (z-order) for correct drawing and interaction.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to call setFilled(true) when expecting a shape’s fill color to appear (setColor only affects outlines unless filled).
Using canvas dimensions (getWidth/getHeight) or object dimensions incorrectly in boundary checks, causing objects to clip or escape the window.
Not calling addMouseListeners/addKeyListeners (or not overriding the correct handler names), resulting in unresponsive input.
tipsForSuccess: Use named constants for sizes, speeds, and timings; this makes tuning animations easier and safer.
Develop incrementally: first draw static objects, then add motion, then add collisions and input, testing after each step.
Visualize debugging info: temporarily draw hitboxes, change colors on collisions, or print positions to track logic errors.

vocabulary.definition: A program type with a drawing canvas and animation utilities like pause().
vocabulary.example: public class MyArt extends GraphicsProgram { public void run() { /* draw here */ } }

vocabulary.definition: Base class for all drawable objects (shapes, images, labels) on the canvas.
A rectangle graphic with width and height.
vocabulary.example: GRect box = new GRect(80, 40); box.setFilled(true); add(box, 100, 100);

vocabulary.definition: An ellipse/circle defined by width and height.
vocabulary.example: GOval ball = new GOval(30, 30); ball.setFilled(true); add(ball, 200, 150);

vocabulary.definition: A straight line between two points.
An image loaded from a file.
A group of GObjects that move together as one.
vocabulary.example: GCompound ship = new GCompound(); ship.add(new GRect(40, 10), 0, 0); add(ship, 100, 300);

vocabulary.definition: Controls whether a shape is filled and its fill color.
Shifts an object by (dx, dy) relative to its current position.
Places an object at an absolute (x, y) position.
Delays the program for a number of milliseconds, enabling smooth animation.
Returns the topmost GObject at a canvas point, or null if none.
The drawing order (front-to-back layering) of objects on the canvas.
Mouse and keyboard handlers that respond to user input.

quiz.question: Where is the origin (0,0) on the GraphicsProgram canvas?
quiz.explanation: ACM Graphics uses a screen coordinate system with (0,0) at the top-left; x increases right, y increases downward.

quiz.question: Which call properly places a GRect r at coordinates (100, 200)?
quiz.explanation: You can place while adding using add(r, x, y). setLocation is fine too, but only after the object is added; option A alone does not add the object.

quiz.question: What does pause(16) in an animation loop most directly control?
quiz.explanation: pause(16) delays about 16 ms per frame, targeting roughly 60 frames per second.

quiz.question: Which method moves a GObject relative to its current position?
quiz.explanation: move(dx, dy) offsets the current position by the given deltas.

quiz.question: To bounce a ball off the right wall, which condition is most appropriate?
quiz.explanation: Check the ball’s right edge: x + width. When it reaches or exceeds the canvas width, reverse dx.

quiz.question: Which call ensures a rectangle’s interior shows the chosen color?
quiz.explanation: You must mark the shape as filled and set its fill color; setColor only changes the outline if not filled.

quiz.question: What is the best way to detect an object directly under a point (x, y)?
options: C) contains(x, y) on every object manually
quiz.explanation: getElementAt(x, y) queries the canvas and returns the topmost GObject at that location.

quiz.question: You wrote an infinite while loop without pause(). What is the most likely symptom?
options: C) The UI becomes unresponsive and animation runs too fast to see
quiz.explanation: Without pause, the loop monopolizes CPU and starves event handling and repainting, freezing the UI.

quiz.question: Which method changes the layering so an object appears above others?
quiz.explanation: sendToFront() places the GObject at the front of the z-order.

quiz.question: You want arrow keys to move a player. What must be done first?
options: A) Call addKeyListeners() and override keyPressed(KeyEvent e)
quiz.explanation: Register for keyboard events with addKeyListeners() and implement keyPressed/keyReleased to handle input.

pseudocodeExamples.title: Bouncing Ball Animation
pseudocodeExamples.description: Shows basic game loop with velocity, wall collisions, and constant frame timing.
pseudocodeExamples.pseudocode:
setup window
create filled circle at center
set dx, dy velocities
loop forever:
  move circle by (dx, dy)
  if circle hits left or right wall: reverse dx
  if circle hits top or bottom wall: reverse dy
  pause for fixed milliseconds

pseudocodeExamples.title: Drag-and-Drop with the Mouse
pseudocodeExamples.description: Demonstrates selecting an object with the mouse and dragging it while preserving the grab offset.
pseudocodeExamples.pseudocode:
add shapes to canvas
register mouse listeners
on mousePressed: record selected object at (x, y) and the offset from object's origin
on mouseDragged: move selected object so that the offset aligns with the current mouse point
on mouseReleased: clear selection

pseudocodeExamples.title: Paddle and Ball Collision (Breakout-style)
pseudocodeExamples.description: Implements a moving ball, user-controlled paddle, and collision response using point sampling and reversing dy.
pseudocodeExamples.pseudocode:
create paddle at bottom, ball near center
set ball dx, dy
register mouse or keyboard for paddle movement
loop forever:
  move ball
  if ball hits left/right walls: reverse dx
  if ball hits top: reverse dy
  if ball passes bottom: reset or stop
  sample points around ball's bottom and sides
  if getElementAt detects paddle/brick: reverse dy and adjust position
  pause fixed time

syntaxReference: Program skeleton: public class MySketch extends GraphicsProgram { public void run() { /* ... */ } }
Add objects: GRect r = new GRect(80,40); add(r, 100, 120);
Colors and fill: rect.setFilled(true); rect.setFillColor(Color.BLUE); rect.setColor(Color.BLACK);
Canvas size: double w = getWidth(); double h = getHeight();
Text: GLabel lbl = new GLabel("Hello"); lbl.setFont("SansSerif-18"); add(lbl, x, y);
Images: GImage img = new GImage("pic.png"); add(img, x, y); img.scale(0.5);
Groups: GCompound c = new GCompound(); c.add(new GOval(20,20), 0, 0); add(c, 50, 50);
Mouse input: addMouseListeners(); public void mousePressed(MouseEvent e) { /* ... */ }
Keyboard input: addKeyListeners(); public void keyPressed(KeyEvent e) { if (e.getKeyCode()==KeyEvent.VK_LEFT) {...} }
Collision sample: GObject hit = getElementAt(x, y); if (hit != null) { /* collide */ }
Fonts and metrics: lbl.getAscent() to baseline-align labels (y is baseline).
commonPatterns: Game loop with velocity: double dx=3, dy=2; while(true){ obj.move(dx,dy); if(hitWall()) dx=-dx; pause(16); }
Drag-and-drop: on mousePressed->selected=getElementAt; store offset; on mouseDragged->selected.setLocation(e.getX()-offX, e.getY()-offY);
Keyboard movement: addKeyListeners(); keyPressed-> if(VK_LEFT) player.move(-SPEED,0); if(VK_RIGHT) player.move(SPEED,0);
Centering object: add(obj, getWidth()/2 - obj.getWidth()/2, getHeight()/2 - obj.getHeight()/2);
Point-sampling collision: GObject hit = getElementAt(ball.getX()+r, ball.getY()+r); if(hit!=null){ dy=-dy; }
Boundary clamp: x = Math.max(0, Math.min(x, getWidth()-obj.getWidth())); obj.setLocation(x, obj.getY());
Parallax/background: background.sendToBack(); move foreground faster than background for depth effect.
Timer alternative: new javax.swing.Timer(16, e->{ step(); }).start(); // if using Swing timer instead of while+pause
quickTips: Use small, consistent time steps (e.g., 16–20 ms) and tune speeds in pixels/frame for smooth motion.
Sample multiple points around fast-moving objects to avoid tunneling through thin paddles or walls.
Keep drawing and game logic separate; write helper methods like updateBall(), checkCollisions(), and handleInput() for clarity.
